column9: Our explainer (patient-facing)

Vitamin D supports bone health, immune function, and muscle strength. Levels below 30 ng/mL are considered insufficient; below 20 is deficient. Adequate sun exposure and supplementation typically correct low levels within 8–12 weeks.
column10: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline on Vitamin D (2024 update)

column9: Vitamin B12 is essential for red blood cell formation and neurological function. Low levels can cause fatigue, numbness, and anaemia; common in vegetarian/vegan diets and in people with gut absorption issues.
column10: National Institutes of Health — Office of Dietary Supplements (2024)

column9: Folate is required for DNA synthesis and red blood cell production. Adequate levels are important for cardiovascular and neurological health.
column10: Institute of Medicine DRI — Folate (2024 review)

column9: Ferritin reflects iron stores. Low levels indicate iron deficiency; high levels can indicate inflammation or iron overload.
Ferritin reflects iron stores. Low levels indicate iron deficiency; high levels can indicate inflammation or iron overload.

TSH is the primary screening test for thyroid function. High TSH typically indicates underactive thyroid; low TSH indicates overactive thyroid. Pregnancy-specific ranges differ significantly — this marker is intentionally not classified when pregnancy status is positive or unknown.
HbA1c estimates average blood sugar over the prior 2–3 months. 5.7–6.4% is prediabetic range; 6.5% or higher meets diabetes threshold. Pregnancy-specific ranges differ.
column10: American Diabetes Association Standards of Care (2024)

column9: Hemoglobin carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body. Low levels indicate anaemia.
column10: WHO anaemia thresholds (2024) + AACC reference intervals

column9: Hemoglobin carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body. Low levels indicate anaemia.
column10: WHO anaemia thresholds (2024) + AACC reference intervals

column9: Free T3 is the active thyroid hormone. Low levels often accompany hypothyroidism; high levels accompany hyperthyroidism. Pregnancy-specific ranges differ.
Free T4 is the inactive storage form of thyroid hormone. Interpreted alongside TSH and Free T3. Pregnancy-specific ranges differ.
Total testosterone drives libido, muscle mass, and hair growth in men. Low levels are associated with fatigue, low libido, and metabolic changes.
Testosterone in women supports bone density, libido, and mood. Elevated levels (outside pregnancy) may indicate PCOS.
Free testosterone is the biologically active fraction unbound to proteins. Interpreted alongside total testosterone and SHBG.
Free testosterone is the biologically active fraction. Elevated levels in women may indicate PCOS.
SHBG binds sex hormones in blood. Low SHBG is associated with insulin resistance; high SHBG with reduced free testosterone.
SHBG binds sex hormones in blood. Low SHBG in women may signal insulin resistance or PCOS.
DHEA-S is a precursor to sex hormones produced by the adrenal glands. Declines with age; low levels are associated with fatigue.
DHEA-S in women supports androgen balance. Elevated levels may indicate adrenal origin of androgen excess.
LH stimulates testosterone production in men. Low levels may indicate pituitary dysfunction.
LH drives ovulation. Elevated LH:FSH ratio is a common marker in PCOS. Pregnancy ranges differ significantly.
FSH supports sperm production in men. Elevated FSH may indicate testicular dysfunction.
FSH drives follicular development. Elevated FSH suggests diminished ovarian reserve or menopause.
Elevated prolactin in men can suppress testosterone production and cause low libido or fertility issues.
Elevated prolactin in non-pregnant women can cause menstrual irregularity. Pregnancy-specific ranges are much higher.
Estradiol in men is normal in small quantities. Elevated levels may indicate aromatase excess.
Estradiol varies dramatically across menstrual cycle phases and life stages. Not classified when pregnancy status is unknown or positive.
AMH reflects ovarian reserve. High levels can indicate PCOS; very low levels suggest diminished reserve.
Serum iron reflects circulating iron. Interpreted with ferritin and transferrin saturation.
Serum iron reflects circulating iron. Pregnancy-specific ranges differ significantly.
Fasting glucose 100–125 mg/dL is prediabetic range; 126+ meets diabetes threshold. Pregnancy thresholds differ.
Fasting insulin reflects baseline insulin production. Elevated levels suggest insulin resistance, often before glucose becomes abnormal.
Total cholesterol is the sum of LDL, HDL, and other lipoproteins. Interpretation requires the full lipid panel.
HDL transports cholesterol away from arteries. Higher is protective.
HDL transports cholesterol away from arteries. Higher is protective.
LDL deposits cholesterol in arteries. High LDL is the primary lipid target for cardiovascular risk reduction.
Triglycerides rise after meals and with carbohydrate-heavy diets. Sustained high levels are cardiovascular risk factors.
ALT rises when liver cells are damaged. Elevated levels may indicate fatty liver, medication injury, or viral hepatitis.
AST is present in liver, heart, and muscle. Interpreted alongside ALT.
Creatinine is a kidney-filtered muscle breakdown product. Elevated levels suggest reduced kidney function.
Creatinine reflects kidney filtration. Lower muscle mass in women gives lower reference range.
Uric acid rises with purine-rich diets and metabolic syndrome. Sustained high levels can precipitate gout.
WBC count reflects immune activity. Elevated counts suggest infection or inflammation; very low counts warrant attention.
Platelets are essential for blood clotting. Low counts can cause bleeding risk; very high counts increase clotting risk.
MCV is the average red blood cell size. Helps classify anaemia: low MCV suggests iron deficiency; high MCV suggests B12/folate deficiency.
